data.title: Summit Therapeutics: Moving Stock To 'Hold' Rating After Phase 3 HARMONi Data Release
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Summit Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SMMT) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Summit Therapeutics: Mid-2025 NSCLC Data Could Further Entrench Ivonescimab Program". With respect to this article, I rated the company a "Strong Buy" and noted that it was gearing up to release data from its phase 3 HARMONi study using its drug ivonescimab in combination with chemotherapy for the treatment of patients with 2nd-line EGFR non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC]. That is, patients who have gone on to fail on 3rd-generation Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors [TKIs]. Based on the clinical data that was released, I am changing the rating of this stock from a "Strong Buy" to a "Hold Rating". As I will show below, the company did well with respect to the primary efficacy endpoint of progression-free survival [PFS] in this phase 3 HARMONi study.

However, it failed to achieve statistical significance with respect to the other primary efficacy endpoint, which is overall-survival [OS]. Why is this important? Well, the reason why is because the FDA noted that a statistically significant OS benefit is necessary for marketing authorization of ivonescimab for the treatment of these 2nd-line EGFR NSCLC patients. With that being said, it is not clear at what point the biotech will now be able to file its Biologics License Application [BLA] of this PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody for the treatment of this patient population. While it is still possible that the OS trend could improve, at this point it remains an unknown. With that being said, this is why I'm changing the rating from a "Strong Buy" to a "Hold". In addition, it will be important to see if the FDA accepts the BLA based on statistical significance being achieved with respect to PFS.

Ivonescimab Mixed Results Brings Up Major Doubt For Marketing Approval In the United States

As I noted above, Summit Therapeutics reported topline results from its phase 3 HARMONi study using its bispecific PD-1/VEGF antibody drug to treat patients with EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC. As I will be going over below, the results were mixed in nature and this brings about major questions on the company's ability to possibly file for U.S. marketing approval. Before going over this late-stage trial data, plus any possible outcomes to come out of it, it is first important to go over what non-small cell lung cancer is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Lung cancer is characterized as the growth of malignant cells that form a tumor on the lung itself. Typically, the cancer starts to form primarily in the cells that line the air passageways of the lungs. Like most other cancers, anyone can develop lung cancer for sure, but it is primarily formed in those who either smoke or those who experience second-hand smoke. There are even some instances of chemicals causing patients to obtain lung cancer as well. Furthermore, this type of cancer is split into two different types, depending upon the aggressive growth nature of each. The two types of this cancer are as follows:

  • Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer [NSCLC]
  • Small-Cell Lung Cancer [SCLC]

With this being said, it is believed that approximately 85% fall into the category of NSCLC. Then, roughly between 15% fall into the zone of having SCLC. I will state that SCLC is highly, more aggressive in nature. While it does respond to radiation and chemotherapy, it still tends to have a higher recurrence rate. Plus, by the time this type of lung cancer is diagnosed, it has already spread to other parts of a patient's body. The global non-small cell lung cancer therapeutics market is expected to reach $37.49 billion by 2035. This is a very large market opportunity, and it is possible that it could go after this entire market depending upon how its other ongoing clinical studies go, but with respect to the phase 3 HARMONi study program, it is targeting a specific group of NSCLC patients. The rate at which a NSCLC patient has the EGFR mutation varies depending upon the region. From one study being done, the pooled evidence of EGFR mutation prevalence was that of 32.3%. Thus, the point here is even if the company targets this specific group of NSCLC patients, it would still be a big market opportunity for it to go after.

In order to see whether or not the ivonescimab combination will be enough to help treat these patients with EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC, the company ran the phase 3 HARMONi study. This specific study recruited up to a total of 420 patients who were randomized 1:1 to receive either of the following treatment dosing regimens as follows:

  • Ivonescimab + Pemetrexed + Carboplatin [chemotherapy combination]
  • Placebo + Pemetrexed + Carboplatin

In essence, the plan was to see if the ivonescimab combination could beat placebo combination in the targeting of these EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC patients. One important item to highlight immediately is that this specific late-stage trial incorporated two primary efficacy endpoints. The first primary endpoint was that of progression-free survival [PFS] and then the second one was that of Overall-Survival [OS]. The primary endpoint of PFS was met quite easily with flying colors. It was revealed that patients given the ivoescimab + chemotherapy combination were able to reduce their risk of disease progression or death by 48% [Hazard ration of 0.52]. This was proven that the company's bispecific PD-1/VEGF antibody was superior to that of chemotherapy alone. The results ended up being mixed because the other important primary efficacy endpoint of OS was trending in a favorable fashion, but did not reach statistical significance. The p-value of this endpoint of OS was that of p=0.057. A positive would be that no FDA-approved therapy has shown an OS benefit in this EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC patient population. On the flip side, the timing of a BLA is in question. That's because after the release of this data, Summit stated that it doesn't have an established timeline yet of when it could possibly file its BLA of ivonescimab for starters. Even more important than this, but there is one important factor to highlight in the press release that was revealed. It was noted in a quote as the following:

"The FDA noted that a statistically significant overall survival benefit is necessary to support marketing authorization, which will weigh into Summit’s considerations regarding the timing of a potential BLA filing"

This here is important because while it is possible that the FDA might allow the biotech to file its BLA of ivonescimab for the treatment of these specific NSCLC patients, there is no assurance that it will. Especially, based on the notion that the company needs to achieve statistical significance when it comes to OS in this patient population. With this in mind, this is why I specifically changed my rating from a "Strong Buy" to a "Hold" rating after this press release. If the trend in OS alters in time to be statistically significant, then this would change the scope of this program. That's when I would change the rating back to a "Strong Buy". For now, the ability for the company to file its BLA of this bispecific PD-1/VEGF antibody remains a huge mystery. Long-term outcomes of western patients to be followed might help the trend in OS improve to be statistically significant, but there is no assurance of this occurring.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Summit Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments of $361.3 million as of March 31st of 2025. The reason for the cash on hand that it has is because of a private placement agreement that was done back in September 2024. This was with respect to the selling of an aggregate of 10.35 million shares of the company's common stock at a price of $22.70 per share. With respect to this offering, the company was able to generate gross proceeds of $235 million. The company is likely going to need to raise additional funds soon though. The reason why this is the case is because of what it notes in its 10-Q SEC filing, which is that it believes it only has enough to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. The company burns approximately $66.8 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $51.2 million in R&D expenses and then $15.6 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Summit Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the release of data from the phase 3 HARMONi study using ivonescimab + chemotherapy to target EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC patients. While the one primary efficacy endpoint of PFS was achieved in a statistically significant manner, the other one of OS was not. This is not ideal because again this puts the timing and potential of a BLA of ivonescimab to the FDA into question. In essence, there is no assurance that this company will be able to obtain U.S. marketing approval of this PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody.

The second risk would be in terms of a submitted BLA filing. If say, for example, the FDA allows the company to file a regulatory application based on PFS alone, the BLA must still go through a review process in terms of components, CMC and totality of safety/efficacy data. Thus, even if the BLA is approved for review, there is no guarantee that the company will achieve U.S. marketing approval of ivonescimab + chemotherapy for the treatment of these EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC patients.

The third and final risk to consider would be that of the ongoing phase 3 HARMONi-3 study, which is using ivonescimab + chemotherapy to treat patients with 1st-line metastatic NSCLC. The ivvonescimab combination is going to be compared to that of KEYTRUDA [pembrolizumab] + chemotherapy for the treatment of these patients. It is important to state that NSCLC patients with genomic alterations are excluded from this study. In essence, those who have EGFR, ALK, ROS1 and BRAF V600E genomic alterations. The risk here is that upon the release of PFS and/or OS data from this phase 3 HARMONi-3 study, there is no assurance that either of these co-primary efficacy endpoints are going to be met in a statistically significant manner.

Conclusion

The company might still have a path forward for marketing approval of ivonescimab in the United States for the targeting of EGFR 2nd-line NSCLC patients, but this remains to be seen. That's because with the OS number not being met with statistical significance, it puts the timing of a BLA of ivonescimab for the treatment of these patients into question. For starters, there is no assurance that this company will even be allowed to file for U.S. regulatory approval of this PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody for the treatment of this specific NSCLC patient population. In addition, there is no assurance that the OS trend observed from the ongoing phase 3 HARMONi study is going to eventually achieve statistical significance over an extended period of time. Until the OS number improves and data is revealed from the phase 3 HARMONi-3 study, I believe that the stock is now a "Hold". If any of these items change over the course of time, then such a rating could shift, but there is no certainty of this happening.

data.title: 3i Group: A Hidden Growth Gem In Europe
data.description:
While I'm an income-focused investor, for my personal retirement portfolio I also look for companies that have strong growth prospects, which should have more upside potential over the long term than income plays. One stock that I currently own and have covered only once on Seeking Alpha several years ago is 3i Group plc (OTCPK:TGOPY), which I personally think is a hidden gem in Europe.

Since my previous analysis back in 2017, its total return has been above 570%, which is quite impressive, especially considering that the overall stock market 'only' returned about 150% during the same period.

While investors may think that following a strong share price rally, there isn't much upside left, I think otherwise, and I still see plenty of growth ahead for the company and upside potential over the long term due to an increasing net asset value. This makes 3i Group a great growth play in Europe.

Business Profile

For investors who aren't aware of what the company does, 3i Group is an investment company based in the United Kingdom, focused mainly on private equity and infrastructure assets. 3i Group invests in mid-sized companies both in Europe and North America having a long-term approach, aiming to generate attractive returns for shareholders and investors. Its current market value is about $53 billion and its shares trade in the U.S. on the over-the-counter market, having relatively good liquidity, at least for retail investors.

3i Group invests its own capital and also manages private equity and infrastructure funds, but the vast majority of its assets under management are proprietary capital. This leads to a good alignment of interests between 3i Group's management team and external investors, being a distinctive factor in the investment management industry.

As shown in the previous graph, 3i Group's total assets under management amounted to £38.7 billion at the end of last March (its fiscal year ends in March), while its investment portfolio had a value of £25.6 billion. In private equity, the business is funded mainly by proprietary capital with some funding from co-investors on specific assets, while on infrastructure it invests its own capital and also manages assets for third-party investors. This means 3i Group's revenues and earnings come from dividends received from its investments, but also from fund management fees.

Contrary to many private equity companies that usually invest for a period of up to ten years, 3i Group has a different approach and stays invested longer than in some specific companies, having truly a long-term approach when managing its investments. A good example is Action, its largest asset, which 3i Group invested back in 2011 and plans to hold for many years ahead.

Indeed, since the company invested in Action, its growth history has been fantastic, as net asset value (NAV) per share has increased by more than 800% since 2012, a strong performance that is largely explained by Action's success story.

Reflecting Action's impressive growth over the past thirteen years, at the end of last fiscal year Action represented about 74% of 3i Group's investment portfolio value, while the second-largest asset was Royal Sanders (another long-term investment), which had a weight of only 3.4% on 3i Group's investment portfolio.

This clearly shows that 3i Group's track record is intertwined with Action, a profile that is not expected to change much in the near future. Beyond Action, the company invests in several sectors, including infrastructure, consumer goods, healthcare, or industrial, but due to Action's heavyweight in its portfolio, the consumer sector is by far the largest one.

Action's Business Profile & Track Record

While investors may at first glance become worried about 3i Group's large concentration on a single asset, I don't think this is an issue because Action is a superb company, and its impressive performance in the past it's not much likely to change in the near future.

So, what is Action?

Action is a non-food discount retailer based in the Netherlands, which was founded in 1993. The company has a simple and standardized format for its stores, offering low prices and a convenient shopping experience for its customers, while about 90% of its product offerings are common across all stores.

3i Group bought the majority of the company back in 2011 from its founders and owns nowadays 57.9% of its shares. Since its investment, 3i Group has supported Action's growth strategy, which has been largely based on expanding its presence throughout Continental Europe and reaching higher penetration of its existing markets.

When 3i Group bought Action, its operations were concentrated on the Benelux, being heavily exposed to its domestic market. 3i Group supported an impressive internationalization process since then, with Action opening new stores in adjacent countries and expanding to new markets in a sustainable way.

Indeed, Action's business model is quite simple and replicable across different countries, and to reduce risk the company opens new stores close to its existing distribution centers. This allows it to 'test' a new market with a limited investment, as the cost of new stores is usually around £0.4 million, while a new distribution center is a much larger investment.

When 3i Group bought Action, the retailer only had about 245 stores and two distribution centers, while at the end of 2024, its total number of stores was close to 3,000 and Action had 15 distribution centers across Europe. Action is nowadays present in some 12 European countries and plans to enter two new countries during 2025, it has plans to build an additional five distribution centers during the next couple of years to support its stores' expansion plans.

This physical space growth has naturally led to strong sales and earnings growth over the past few years, which were achieved fully organically. Action's growth track record is quite impressive, showing that its business model is replicable across different countries, and has remained successful as the company gets larger.

As can be seen in the previous graph, Action's sales compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) was around 24% over the past two decades, while its EBITDA growth is even stronger (CAGR of 28%) as the business has achieved operating efficiencies due to its larger size.

While Action is now much larger than it was some years ago, its annual growth continues to be impressive given that sales increased by 22% YoY during 2024, and EBITDA was up by 29% YoY. This is a fantastic achievement for a company that already generated more than €11 billion in sales during 2023, showing that its growth is not showing signs of slowing down.

Action's Growth Prospects

Despite an impressive growth history, Action's growth prospects are still quite strong as the company continues to invest in business growth, by opening new stores and building additional distribution centers.

Distribution centers are critical for its expansion plans, as the company's business model is based on centralized and efficient distribution, reducing logistics costs and offering new products every week to customers as an incentive for them to come back into stores. This business model has allowed the company to outperform the industry's growth over the past five years, a trend that is expected to persist in the coming years.

As shown in the previous graph, Action has outperformed the retail industry in Europe, both due to its expansion efforts and market share gains, allowing it to grow faster than peers. This is expected to continue ahead supported by its growth strategy, as Action is now in the second phase of its store's expansion plan.

While during 2012-2021, its expansion was performed mainly across Central European countries, such as large countries like France or Germany, Action has been in more recent years expanding to Southern European countries, like Italy, Spain, and Portugal. This means Action is now present in European countries covering a population of almost 400 million people, offering significant growth opportunities ahead as Action's penetration across most markets is still relatively low.

For the countries the company currently considers to be "in-scope," it sees additional potential of about 4,850 stores, showing that Action's growth potential over the coming years is still quite strong. As can be seen in the next graph, Action opened 352 stores during 2024 (+13.7% YoY), setting a new annual record, and entered a new market (Portugal), while it plans to enter Switzerland and Romania in 2025, plus Croatia and Slovenia during 2026. It expects to open about 370 new stores during 2025, being another record year for the company and strong support for growth ahead.

Going forward, I think it's possible that Action may open around 400 stores per year. This means that at this opening pace, Action may take about 12 years to reach its targeted store density, which can be further increased as the company considers new geographies to operate in the future.

Despite these somewhat aggressive growth plans, the company has a very sensible expansion strategy, entering new markets that can be fulfilled by existing distribution centers. Switzerland can easily be supplied by distribution centers based in France, while Romania's supplies may come from Poland or the Czech Republic. When operations in new countries are more mature, it eventually decides to invest in more local distribution centers, limiting the risk of entering new markets.

Beyond new countries, Action still has good growth prospects in countries where it already has operations, namely in Italy, Spain, and Portugal, where its penetration is very low compared to other market such as the Netherlands, France, or Germany. For instance, in my home country, Portugal, Action entered the market in 2024 by opening a store in the northern part of the country. Thereafter, it opened an additional nine stores, all being supplied by its distribution center located in Spain.

This allows the company to test the market and see how good the customer reaction and sales per store are, potentially expanding to other regions if the market reaction is positive. Where I live (in the South part of Lisbon) there is no Action store and this is a region that has a population of close to one million people, showing that Action has great growth prospects in Portugal.

Indeed, the larger Lisbon area (represented in the map below) is the most populated in the country with about 2.8 million people, but there are only two Action stores (the blue dots) in this region. This clearly shows how untapped the retail market is in Portugal for the company (which has close to 11 million people), boding quite well for Action to open new stores in the country over the next few years.

However, despite Action's strong growth prospects over the medium to long term as the company continues its expansion strategy both in existing and new markets, due to its larger size, I think it's natural that growth rates will gradually come down over the next few years. For instance, while Action is expected to open a new record number of stores during 2025, its new store annual growth rate should decline to 12.7% (vs. 13.7% in 2024), and I think it is quite likely that annual sales and earnings growth should decline to below 20% over the coming years as the company becomes larger, which remains nonetheless quite good within the European retail sector.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, 3i Group's earnings and returns have been quite strong over the past few years, supported by Action and also by its other investments. As Action has delivered very strong sales and earnings growth, its valuation has increased significantly since 3i Group invested in it and has also made large distributions to its owners.

Indeed, 3i Group's carrying value for its Action stake was valued at more than £17.8 billion at the end of last March (FY 2025), representing an annual increase of 26%. This strong performance is justified by Action's operating performance given that 3I Group has maintained its EBITDA multiple valuation unchanged since 2020, at about 18.5x EBITDA.

Action's valuation multiple seems to be somewhat conservative considering its growth history and strong business prospects ahead, it's also lower than compared to other discount store operators that are trading at multiples above 20x. Beyond Action, there was also some value increase in other investments, even though the largest gain by far was related to Action.

Given this backdrop, it's not surprising to see that 3i Group's NAV increased by 22% YoY to £25.42 per share at the end of FY 2025, driven by higher values of its private equity investments. Its return on equity (ROE) ratio was 25% in FY 2025, being a very good level of profitability, showing that 3i Group has a quality portfolio of investments that offer strong growth and returns at the same time.

As 3i Group is an investment company, its cash inflows come from dividends received by its equity stakes, of which the dividend received from Action is by far the largest inflow. In FY 2025, 3i Group received £433 million from Action (+15.5% YoY), which represented 92% of its cash inflows from private equity stakes. The infrastructure segment also distributes significant cash (£106 million last year), leading to a total cash income of £598 million in FY 2025. This was a slight increase compared to the previous fiscal year, as higher dividends received from Action were offset by lower dividends from other investments in the private equity segment following about £0.6 billion of asset realizations during the last year.

This cash income was more than enough to cover 3i Group's operating cash expenses of £129 million, resulting in an operating cash profit of £469 million in FY 2025 (+0.4% YoY). The company's net debt was only around £800 million at the end of last March, representing 3.1% of its NAV, showing that 3i Group's leverage position is quite conservative (its target is to maintain leverage below 5% of NAV over the medium term), allowing it to distribute a growing dividend to shareholders.

Related to its FY 2025 earnings, 3i Group increased its dividend to £0.73 per share, up by 19.7% YoY, but at its current share price, it only offers a dividend yield of about 1.8%. This is relatively low and shows that 3i Group's income appeal is not fantastic, making its investment case clearly more geared towards growth rather than income.

Valuation & Risks

Regarding its valuation, considering that 3i Group is an investment company, the best metric to analyze is, in my opinion, the price-to-NAV multiple (P/NAV). As 3i Group's growth should remain quite strong ahead, its NAV per share is expected to grow to about £30.4 per share by the end of FY 2026, leading to a forward P/NAV multiple of 1.35x.

Over the past couple of years, 3i Group has traded between 1.4x-1.6x NAV, showing that its current valuation is attractive for long-term investors. However, I'm not expecting 3i Group to trade at much higher multiples than around 1.5x NAV over the coming months; therefore, upside potential should come mostly from NAV growth ahead rather than a re-rating of its shares. Indeed, NAV growth has been the major driver of higher share prices in the past, a trend that I expect to be maintained over the next few years.

Regarding risks, 3i Group is largely exposed to a single asset (Action) making it highly dependent on Action's performance to maintain its growth profile in the future. In the short term, a weaker macroeconomic outlook in Europe may negatively impact consumer sentiment and Action's organic sales growth, which could lead to a growth slowdown that eventually may not be well received by investors. Additionally, competitors may be more aggressive on pricing and may also have expansion plans into Action's existing footprint, potentially negatively impacting its growth in the near term.

Over the medium to long term, a slowdown in new store expansion is a risk that investors should not overlook, as Action is nowadays present in most Western European countries and further growth prospects in large countries like France and Germany are smaller than compared to some years ago. While Action still has a lot of growth potential in Western Europe, its expansion plans in Southern and Eastern Europe increases its exposure to countries with weaker economies and more unstable political regimes (for instance Romania), something that may eventually hurt its financial performance in the future.

Moreover, Action has performed some research about entering new markets, including the U.K. and Asia, but has decided not to go ahead for the time being. The company is still exploring the possibility of entering the U.S. market, which could significantly boost its growth prospects over the long term, but it's a risky move to enter a completely new geography. Additionally, the investment required to establish operations from scratch can be considerable, being somewhat uncertain if Action's business model would work or not in the U.S.

While I think Action can maintain business growth rates between 15-20% per year over the next decade by being just focused on Europe, over the long-term Action's growth should naturally decline if the company doesn't decide to enter a new continent. Action's expansion into geographies where it doesn't have any track record will certainly be riskier, and its business model may not work as it does in Europe, potentially putting its long-term growth prospects at risk.

Conclusion

3i Group plc is one of the best "growth" plays in Europe in my opinion, supported by its main asset Action which has a very strong business model in the hard discount retail market. Its success story over the past decade is quite incredible, beating my own expectations when I covered 3i Group back in 2017. Despite its strong track record, I still see tremendous growth opportunities ahead, as Action's expansion plans should lead to sustainable NAV growth for many years ahead. Despite this attractive setback, 3i Group's current valuation seems to be attractive for long-term investors, making it a great growth pick in the European financial services sector right now.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dynex Capital: Conditionally Bullish
data.description:
Dynex Capital, Inc. (NYSE:DX), a mortgage REIT currently yielding near 17% is covered in today's analysis. The objective of our coverage derives from three factors, namely, explaining Dynex's strategy, suggesting why its yield shouldn't be looked at in isolation, and providing ideas on how to isolate the yield without surrendering to excess volatility or expectations mismatch.

We last covered Dynex in February, assigning a bullish rating on the basis of overstated implied prepayment rates, its compelling yield and a reduction in refinancing risk. We've decided to reiterate our outlook; however, we are doing so based on a few conditions.

Herewith is an explanation of our thought process and why we decided on a conditional bullish outlook.

Dynex: A Fundamental Review

Starting with Dynex's performance. The time series below illustrates that it has produced positive total returns in the past ten years, primarily deriving from distributions as its total returns have settled in negative territory. As shown by the kinks in the distribution, Dynex can suffer from abrupt declines during higher stress environments, especially when option-adjusted spreads (credit spreads) spike. This fact was communicated earlier this year when the reciprocal tariff debacle resulted in high-yielding OAS spiking to around 461 basis points, leading Dynex substantially lower. Despite being linked to mortgage rates, the OAS-to-Dynex return relationship shows that the vehicle is often adverse to high-risk environments.

Now, let's look at Dynex's latest fundamental performance while blending in explanations of its strategy.

Dynex uses short-term funding to buy longer-term mortgages. To our understanding, it relies on pledging lower-yielding residential and commercial mortgages as collateral to obtain short-term funding, which it subsequently invests in longer-term RMBS and CMBS products. Its strategy has delivered mixed quarter-over-quarter results. Per-share data demonstrates that it traversed into a net income per share loss of (6 cents), largely due to a negative ($1.48) adjustment in derivative fair values. Moreover, Dynex's book value per share receded by 14 cents and comprehensive income remained flat at 16 cents per share. On the plus side, total economic returns grew by 160 basis points to 2.6%, doing so with a lower quarter-over-quarter leverage ratio of 7.4x.

A more detailed look at Dynex's portfolio raises interesting talking points, especially from a quarter-over-quarter vantage point. Based on asset type percentages, the portfolio remained grossly the same regarding its agency versus non-agency mortgages, Dynex remains focused on guaranteed mortgages with non-agency mortgages (mostly CMBS) shaping less than 0.2% of its portfolio. Furthermore, agency CMBS exposure remained at 2%, indicating that Dynex remains agency RMBS centric. The To-be-Announced pool settled slightly higher at 25% versus a previous 24% – For those unaware, TBA means the mortgages are priced in the forward market as opposed to upon acquisition; it adds a speculative component. While mortgage types remained grossly the same, Dynex shifted its quarter-over-quarter coupon exposure, reducing its 5% coupon target from 31% to 27%, concurrently shaping a more staggered portfolio with a slight drift into 4% coupons, where the exposure changed from 8% to 14% quarter-over-quarter. It's worth noting that mortgage rates settled lower during the first quarter; therefore, the lower coupon allocation seems market-driven, we might see that a reversal occurred during Q2 when Dynex releases its results, mainly due to the fact that mortgage rates have reignited on the basis of higher U.S. treasury yields.

A widened view of Dynex displays that interest income increased during its first quarter, mainly due to lower interest expenses. Whether this will sustain into late 2025 and beyond remains debatable; let's move into the next section to discuss the various possibilities.

Market Commentary

Asset-Level

Duration Effect On Prices

Before discussing duration effects, I need to mention the mortgage spread and how mortgage rates can move in tandem or dislocate from treasury yields. It's quite common to look at a mortgage spread as the difference between 30-year agency RMBS rates and the U.S. 10-year treasury yield. As shown in the following chart, when treasury yields shift, you'll usually see mortgage rates move in tandem, mainly because base interest rates are higher. However, in some instances, the spread can widen. For example, mortgage lenders may consider the credit cycle worrisome and therefore enhance the risk premium on mortgages, in turn, leading to a higher spread. Alternatively, the environment can be perceived as less risky, leading to a lower spread. As things stand, the spread has widened materially since 2022's rate-hiking cycle started; however, year-over-year spreads remain range-bound – I calculated the spread on May 22nd 2025 as 243 basis points, slightly lower that a year earlier where spreads settled at 247 basis points (on May 23rd 2024).

Aside: Here's a link to the chart I created if you're interested in exact mortgage and treasury rate numbers.

Now, let's get started with duration and what that could mean for Dynex leading forward.

Duration is a broad term for price sensitivity. Conventionally, effective duration (an option-adjusted method), measures the price sensitivity of an asset for a parallel shift in rates, whereby positive duration suggests an inverse relationship and vice versa. For example, if rates shift down by 100 basis points across the yield curve and your duration is 6, we'd expect a 6% price increase in the asset's value.

While duration isn't perfect, it provides valuable inferences. A study by Yihai Yu, published by MSCI, investigated the effects of duration on mortgage prices given the long-standing debate that mortgages lose value when rates fall due to prepayment risk, lower interest accrued, and refinancing risks. Yu used COVID-19 as a base case to substantiate longer-term qualitative findings, showing that duration is mostly positive, even when interest rates fall. In fact, you'd probably only see negative duration occur when severe rate cuts are implemented, which usually coincide with economic struggles.

Yu's study provides useful evidence, which we incorporated with our base knowledge to determine Dynex's trajectory; a discussion follows the diagram.

We've argued for months that U.S. yields are too high. Our base case is that the economy is soft and will continue to soften, which would compress real rates, level inflation, and lead investors into safer assets such as U.S. treasuries. Our forecast is that 10-year yields are justified between 400 and 410 basis points and that 30-year yields (also relevant to Dynex) are justified between 408 and 420 basis points – here's a link to our article on TLT ETF (TLT) where we explained our reasoning.

Given our rate view, we think Dynex might gain in value due to a positive duration effect and lower financing rates (elaborated upon later). However, we have doubts about the certainty of the price increase due to possible duration shortening during a lower yield cycle, increased prepayments, higher economic risks, and whether mortgage rates will follow or dislocate from treasury yields. As such, we are torn between Dynex's interim price prospects and would prefer hedging a portion of its price risk component (discussed later) as opposed to committing to directionality.

Here's a 10-year view of Dynex's price level versus 30-year mortgage rates, which shows an inverse relationship with occasional dislocations.

Current Yields & Income Effect

Mortgage rates are high and Dynex isolates upper percentile of coupons. The vehicle has a 4-year average dividend yield of 11.63%, a forward dividend yield of 16.93%, and a history shows that its lowest annualized average yield since 2015 is 8.68%, which occurred shortly after the COVID-19 pandemic, where mortgage rates were exceptionally low. In our view, Dynex will pay commendable dividends for the foreseeable future despite the potential scenario of mortgage rate declines.

Financing

As previously mentioned, Dynex uses collateralized short-term funding to buy longer-term assets, which are commonly known as repo or carry trades. The fund borrowed at a rate of 4.46% during Q1. However, we think rates will drop in due course as our outlook on federal funds rates is that a 75 basis point cut within the next twelve months remains likely. We think secure overnight funding rates will follow suit, leading to a lower overall funding base for Dynex. SOFR is currently at around 431 basis points, down by circa 9 basis points since the start of the trading year. SOFR rates ranged between 150 basis and 200 basis points shortly before COVID-19. Though we don't see a return to that level occurring in the next twelve months, we definitely see migration more towards that being a strong probability.

A potential funding-related risk relates to haircuts, which, according to Dynex's 10-Q means:

If the fair value of the collateral falls below the amount required by the lender, the lender has the right to demand additional margin or collateral. These demands are referred to as “margin calls,” and if we fail to meet any margin call, our lenders have the right to terminate the repurchase agreement and sell any collateral pledged. The weighted average haircut for our borrowings as of March 31, 2025, was consistent with prior periods, typically averaging less than 5% for borrowings collateralized with Agency RMBS and CMBS and between 10-14% for borrowings collateralized with CMBS IO.

Haircuts can increase when the economy is shaky because lenders often devalue collateral with higher risk premiums and/or lower market values. If economic risk intensifies, we could see haircuts spike, in turn, leading to lower leverage accessibility. In addition, economic risk leads to higher counterparty risk, meaning, the lender's own credit worthiness comes into play.

Hedging

Dynex selectively hedges interest rate risk with treasury futures and interest rate swaps. We applaud the methodology because it's a clever way to stay nimble and adjust dynamically. That said, based on our own rate trading experience, we have identified a few factors worth outlining; a discussion follows the diagram.

Here are a few factors worth considering.

  • You'll have basis risk when hedging a product with a differentiated instrument, even if that instrument is correlated. In simple terms, this means that correlations can break down between MBS and interest rate products, making the hedging program inefficient at times.
  • Hedging short-term rates can work if you're interested in protecting a strategy from tail risk or dislocations. However, treasury futures can be exceptionally volatile, and interest rate swaps can be slow to unwind. These attributes are somewhat reflected in Dynex's market behavior, which shows that it suffered a three-year max drawdown of 38.57%, implying that its tail hedging strategy possess limitations.

'Valuation' Metrics

We mainly have a systematic outlook of Dynex. Nonetheless, like all assets, its price level needs to be considered relative to its fundamentals. Dynex traded at a market value of $12.05 upon authoring this article. I amalgamated its past four quarters' book values per share and embedded a table below, which shows that Dynex has a twelve-month trailing price-to-book ratio of around 0.95x. Though its book value may shift throughout the next year, we think the metric shows that Dynex isn't dislocated from its fundamental worth. In fact, considering its yield, one could argue that it remains investable.

Period P/B Ratio (per common share)
Q1 2025 $12.56
Q4 2024 $12.70
Q3 2024 $13.00
Q2 2024 $12.50
Average $12.69

Sources: Dynex

Our Conditions For A Bullish Thesis

We are generally quite bullish about Dynex's prospects but under certain conditions. This is a very complex product with severe price volatility and therefore, we laid out a few conditions that we would apply if we were to invest in Dynex. We acknowledge that some might disagree, which is fair. However, these conditions are merely part of our own opinion.

1) Isolating Income And Tactically Using Price Uncertainty

The vehicle's price outlook arguably remains highly uncertain, meaning we're mostly interested in capturing Dynex's income component. Unless we have real conviction with our pricing outlook, we'd probably see any price gains as a bonus. Alternatively, we would risk price losses given under the condition that we can offset capital gains elsewhere in our portfolio for tax reasons. In that way, we are using Dynex tactically as opposed to committing in isolation.

2) Partially Hedging Price Risk

I ran a correlation analysis between Dynex's price behavior and various other assets. I discovered two meaningful hedges, namely Simplify Interest Rate Hedge ETF (PFIX) and ProShares UltraShort 20+ Year Treasury (TBT). Rolling correlations show negative relationships, allowing us to limit Dynex's price behavior while still collecting income from PFIX and TBT, which have trailing yields of 3.19% and 4.19% apiece (yields are subject to change). For those unaware, PFIX is an interest rate vehicle that hedges against higher U.S. rates, whereas TBT is a short long-term treasury ETF, providing -2x the return of the ICE U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Bond Index. By isolating their market values (not distributions), both essentially speculate that long-term treasury yields will spike.

Aside: We didn't correlate total return because 1) we are looking to hedge price risk, and 2) different payout dates can impair the correlation.

The time series above shows that TBT ETF's correlation coefficient is quite volatile and that PFIX's data availability is limited. Thus, we should admit that hedging inconsistencies can occur. Our choice in hedging would be PFIX ETF due to its stronger negative correlation with Dynex and purer focus on rate levels. The following diagram shows a hedging ratio that I calculated, conveying that buying 0.51 units of PFIX-to-Dynex throughout the cycle would likely hedge one's price risk. Of course, my calculations can be subject to error and the hedge ratio's volatility means an investor won't be perfectly hedged at all times. Nevertheless, the finding probably serves as a helpful indicator.

Conclusion

We remain bullish about Dynex Capital's prospects, conditional on the fact that it is placed into a well-structured portfolio that either hedges some of its price risk or acknowledges that Dynex's risk-return outlook can be unstable at times.

In isolation, we expect Dynex's funding rates to drop in due course. Though its asset-level returns might compress in tandem, we still foresee a positive earnings spread being sustained. Furthermore, we admire the vehicle's yield, nimble hedging strategy, and its focus on high-coupon agency RMBS.

Consensus: Bullish

data.title: Itepekimab Failure A Setback For Sanofi And Regeneron
data.description:
Shares of Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) and its partner Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:REGN) are down today after the companies announced that itepekimab met the primary endpoint in only one of the two phase 3 trials in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease ("COPD"). The results were inconsistent across trials and across time points, and this does not look like an approvable or competitive dataset.

While this is not anywhere close to a death blow to either company, it is a considerable setback considering the previous expectations of itepekimab becoming a $2-3 billion drug for Regeneron and Sanofi by the early 2030s, and the outcome should result in downward revisions to revenue and EPS estimates for both companies.

Itepekimab's results from the AERIFY phase 3 program

Itepekimab was successful in the AERIFY-1 trial, where its every two-week and four-week regimens showed a 30% and 34% reduction in moderate or severe exacerbations at week 24 and 27% and 21% reductions at week 52.

The AERIFY-2 trial showed an 18% and 21% benefit at week 24, but only a 2% and 12% benefit at week 52, and this trial failed to meet the primary endpoint with statistical significance.

Even the first trial showed weaker results compared to the phase 2 trial, where itepekimab reduced exacerbations by 42%.

The safety profile of itepekimab looks good, but that does not help much.

Sanofi said that the total number of exacerbations was lower than anticipated and that it decreased the power of both trials to detect a statistically significant efficacy signal, and that enrollment mostly occurred during the COVID-19 pandemic, which could have contributed to the overall exacerbation rates.

Sanofi and Regeneron said they:

“remain committed to our broader itepekimab development program and learnings from the AERIFY program will be invaluable as we continue to advance itepekimab in respiratory diseases with unmet need.”

Itepekimab does not seem dead, but at the very least, this is a multi-year setback for the program as this dataset does not look like something that will get it across the regulatory finish line. I now expect no revenue contribution from itepekimab at least until 2029 or 2030 versus previous expectations for a 2026 launch and a significant ramp in the 2027-2030 period.

I would also lower the probability of success in future COPD trials based on the results from the AERIFY program and potential peak sales as even the reduction in exacerbations in the AERIFY-1 trial makes itepekimab less competitive versus previous expectations that were based on the better-looking phase 2 results.

Implications for Regeneron

Itepekimab would have had some overlap with Dupixent, which was recently successfully launched for the treatment of COPD, and it would have probably cannibalized a part of Dupixent's net sales. As such, I expect Dupixent net sales to be somewhat higher without it having to compete with itepekimab in COPD. Of course, this will not be anywhere close to making up for lost net sales of itepekimab.

As a reminder, itepekimab falls under the antibody collaboration between Regeneron and Sanofi, which also covers Dupixent and Kevzara.

Regeneron counted on itepekimab as an important growth driver in the second part of the decade, and so did I, as described in several of my articles, including last year after Dupixent’s phase 3 success in COPD. I was repeatedly talking about itepekimab as one of the key growth drivers in the following years, and now, this part of the story is gone.

Separately, I am becoming concerned about Regeneron’s business development strategy. The management team has been really good at creating a strong biotech company with a diversified pipeline, but becoming a big biopharma company is the part where they seem stuck. The deals we have seen the company make amount to:

  • Acquisitions of small companies on the brink of collapse, such as Checkmate Pharmaceuticals for $250 million in 2022 and Decibel for $109 million in 2023, and more recently, a genetic testing company 23andMe out of bankruptcy for $256 million.
  • Early-stage collaborations with modest financial commitments like Alnylam (ALNY) for $800 million upfront and Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) that are still far from generating revenues.

I am not saying the company should go out and buy something expensive just for the sake of making a deal, but with biotech stocks under pressure in recent months, there are more than a few bargains out there, and Regeneron has a robust balance sheet and cash flows that it can put to good use instead of just buying back shares and letting the increasing cash pile sit on the balance sheet.

However, even with this setback in mind and the other recent one – the Q1 Eylea miss with worsening outlook, and the above-mentioned business development criticism, I continue to see Regeneron as well positioned for long-term value creation as I believe the market has overcorrected for these setbacks and is now treating the company as one with an expected long-term revenue contraction.

Implications for Sanofi

Sanofi has a broad immunology pipeline and should absorb this setback without significant problems in the following years. As mentioned in the Regeneron section, the lost revenues on itepekimab should be partly offset by a greater focus on Dupixent, but this will not be anywhere close enough to compensate for the loss of itepekimab.

The situation at Sanofi is far less urgent than at Regeneron, as the near and medium-term revenue and EPS outlook looks much better, but similarly, I would like to see Sanofi put some of the cash it received from the recent Opella spinoff to good use and expand its product portfolio and pipeline in the following quarters to compensate for the loss of itepekimab.

And the winner is… Verona Pharma

I am not covering Verona Pharma (VRNA), but believe it deserves a mention here because it came out as a clear winner after itepekimab’s disappointing phase 3 results.

The market had substantial doubts about Ohtuvayre’s competitive prospects against Dupixent at launch and itepekimab down the road, but Verona is so far proving doubters wrong numbers in the first few quarters on the market, and the outlook for Ohtuvayre has now improved after itepekimab’s failure.

Conclusion

Itepekimab’s disappointing phase 3 results are a more significant setback for Regeneron than Sanofi, and a negative market reaction is understandable. The two companies remain committed to the program, but the results are disappointing, and I see a launch delay of 3–4 years and lower annual peak sales potential as the best-case scenario in COPD.

I believe both companies will weather the storm and be able to create shareholder value without itepekimab in COPD, but I would like to see a more proactive business development and pipeline expansion and/or optimization strategy from both companies and especially from Regeneron.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Silver-To-Gold Ratio Is Oversold, But There's More To The Story
data.description:
The Silver/Gold ratio is oversold, but silver is not "undervalued" relative to gold based merely on price

A common hue and cry from Team Silver Bug is that silver is extremely undervalued relative to its big brother in metallic monetary arms. It has to be because its price is so cheap as measured in gold!

Not true. Silver's price relative to gold is what the market and history - save for a few distinct upside explosions in silver and the Silver/Gold ratio - say it is. These dramatic upside events have largely been driven by macro considerations, not silver seeking out its true value before being pounded back down by some elaborate conspiracy.

It is unhealthy to put your unquestioning belief and your capital into such theories. All too often in financial markets (precious metals and crypto in particular) analysis is written as if for children, or at least, for biased eyeballs. Ghost stories can be entertaining and interesting reads, but they are emotional and in the markets we need to be cold, ice-cold, in order to make the right decisions. For that, you need indicators.

Silver is mined differently than gold, often as a by-product metal, has different supply/demand fundamentals, more industrial utility and thus, cyclical aspects, less monetary weight, and is more sensitive to cyclical inflationary macro phases than gold.

The Silver/Gold ratio and its flip side, the Gold/Silver ratio, are macro indicators to me and not a whole lot more. They do indicate whether silver is strong or weak relative to gold, but "strong" and "weak" are not "overvalued" and "undervalued". I have discussed gold's long-term and ongoing "value" proposition many times in the past.

Silver/Gold Ratio: Daily, Weekly and Monthly Charts

Lately, silver is weak relative to gold to the tune of an oversold reading, from which the Silver/Gold ratio is attempting a bottom as it forms a pattern with RSI and MACD diverging positively.

Expanding the view to a weekly chart, we are evaluating whether or not the sharp decline in April will precede an upside event as it did in 2020 and from what level such an event will erupt. The Silver/Gold ratio does tend to react violently to the upside after extreme downside events.

The question is whether weekly RSI became oversold enough to instigate a big upside event. Think of the ratio as a slingshot. The further it pulls back, the more velocity and distance of the snap back.

In the very big picture, we can see that the Silver/Gold ratio has a habit of following up steep declines (red boxes) with strong or extended rallies. It is still in question whether the ratio has become quite oversold enough by monthly RSI. But the risk/reward situation favors silver over gold, regardless of whether the ratio has bottomed yet. Oh, and there is the fact that the ratio is bobbing around multi-decade lows.

In keeping emotions at bay and viewing the ratio as a major market indication with a clear story to tell, I keep ghost stories out of the picture and rational stories of what the future likely holds in the picture.

The Silver/Gold ratio indicator shows risk/reward in favor of silver and thus a whole raft of markets and assets (detailing of which is beyond the scope of this article, but think "commodities") that would benefit from silver rising stronger than gold.

data.title: Nvidia's $8 Billion China Setback: A Speed Bump On An AI Superhighway
data.description:
I don't think anybody would disagree if I said that Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has been the undisputed king of the AI revolution, riding a wave of incredible demand for its GPUs to power everything from data centers to autonomous vehicles. Even those bullish two-three years ago would have probably never imagined that Nvidia could have sustained the utterly insane growth figures it's been publishing quarter after quarter.

However, in its latest Q1 FY2026 earnings report, released on Wednesday after the closing bell, a geopolitical storm cloud loomed large: tightened U.S. export controls on chips to China, resulting in a $4.5 billion loss in Q1 revenue and an expected $8 billion hit in Q2.

Yet, despite this blow, Nvidia delivered jaw-dropping results, beating revenue expectations and issuing stellar Q2 guidance, therefore once again proving its resilience and relentless momentum.

So, for me, the question isn’t whether Nvidia can weather this storm, but it’s how much higher this juggernaut can climb if the so-called headwind were to ease over time on top of that relentless momentum. So let’s take a look at the "China challenge," Nvidia's class act response, and why worries about this headwind seem overblown.

The Pre-Context: China’s Role in Nvidia’s Ascent

Now, for context, China has been a cornerstone of Nvidia's growth, particularly in its Data Center segment, which drives the AI infrastructure for hyperscalers and enterprises worldwide. In recent years, the China AI boom we have seen, fueled by companies like DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) and ByteDance (BDNCE), has resulted in billions of dollars' worth of demand for Nvidia’s GPUs, even as U.S. export controls began to tighten.

To comply, Nvidia developed China-specific chips like the H20, which raked in $4.6 billion in Q1 FY2026 alone before the latest restrictions hit. These chips, less powerful than Nvidia’s flagship Hopper and Blackwell platforms, were a workaround to maintain market access while adhering to U.S. rules.

But if we are being real, the U.S.-China tech tensions have been heating up for years, and it’s been a growing headache for Nvidia. Back in 2022, the U.S. started clamping down on exports of advanced chips to China, pointing to national security risks tied to AI’s potential military uses. Fast forward to Q1 FY2026, and the restrictions hit a new level (i.e., a full-on ban that even included the H20 chips), wiping out a significant revenue stream. Nvidia's overall report, however, tells a different story. Let's examine.

Q1 FY2026: A $4.5 Billion Hit, Yet Record-Breaking Results

As evident in the stock's post-earnings rally, Nvidia's Q1 earnings were nothing short of spectacular, even with the China headwind. The company reported revenue of $44.06 billion, beating Wall Street’s $43.31 billion estimate and marking a fantastic 69% year-over-year.

The Data Center segment (i.e., the company's cash cow) delivered $39.1 billion, up 73% from last year, now accounting for 88% of total revenue. Notably, this growth occurred despite the $4.5 billion charge tied to the H20 ban, which resulted in a $2.5 billion decline in China revenue for the quarter. Without this charge, the GAAP gross margin would have been 71.3%, but even with it, the company posted a very impressive 61%.

Management’s Confidence Says It All

Management's confidence during the earnings call added another layer of assurance regarding this matter. CEO Jensen Huang addressed the China challenge head-on during that time, acknowledging the export controls as a significant headwind but, at the same time, emphasizing Nvidia's ability to adapt. He stated that the company is experiencing strong growth in other regions (Europe, Japan, and the Middle East) and that they are capitalizing on those opportunities.

Huang also hinted at potential relief under the incoming Trump administration. He noted the company is optimistic about policy shifts that could open new pathways. And look, he didn’t elaborate, but if I had to bet, I would argue this suggests Nvidia is lobbying for relaxed controls. Alternatively, Nvidia is exploring alternative markets to offset the loss, which we know is true given the rising demand in other regions.

Another point that Mr. Huang made, which I found quite intriguing, was the success of the H20 chip before the ban, noting its $4.6 billion contribution in Q1 as evidence of Nvidia’s ability to innovate within regulatory constraints. He also pointed to the Blackwell platform, now in full production, as a cornerstone of future growth. He said that demand for Blackwell is incredible, citing deployments of “hundreds of thousands” of GB200 GPUs by Microsoft (MSFT) and other hyperscalers. The demand driven by global markets and next-generation technology seems to position Nvidia well to offset the China loss with minimal disruption.

Q2 FY2026 Guidance: Momentum That Outshines Challenges

Nvidia’s Q2 FY2026 guidance is a testament to Mr. Huang's comments and the overall implication that China won't be an issue moving forward. The company projects revenue of $45 billion, plus or minus 2%, implying at least 60% year-over-year growth despite the projected $8 billion China revenue loss.

It's quite crazy, in my view, that this figure exceeded analyst expectations of $44 billion even with such a massive hit. But importantly, it reflected Nvidia's confidence in scaling Blackwell production and capturing global AI demand.

Another point most investors seem to ignore, reading some of the latest analyses posted, is that the non-GAAP gross margins are expected to remain around 70%, which signals operational efficiency even under pressure and rebound to the sky-high net margins that Nvidia has gotten us used to.

Mr. Huang's comments shed further light on this matter as he emphasized that AI is essentially reshaping whole industries, from healthcare (e.g., Siemens Healthineers (FWB: SHL) adopting Nvidia's AI Enterprise 5.0) to logistics and beyond. The guidance also shows that Nvidia can redirect supply to high-demand regions like the Middle East, where projects like the UAE’s Stargate AI campus with OpenAI and Oracle (ORCL) are gaining traction without giving away any steam. So again, we get reassurance that Nvidia’s growth trajectory remains intact, even with a major market partially closed off.

The Valuation Is Attractive With Consensus Estimates On The Rise

In the meantime, even following the post-earnings share price surge, I find NVDA stock quite attractively priced. The forward multiples don't seem rich at all, considering the underlying earnings growth expectations, and more importantly, the fact that consensus estimates have been on the rise since the report came out. At 32 times this fiscal year's EPS and 24 times next year's estimate, I would even argue that the stock is cheap for a behemoth that is literally both frontrunning and defining the AI revolution.

Notably, these estimates themselves could be quite conservative. We have seen Wall Street consistently revise its estimates upwards. As you can see in the chart below, even though today's estimate for the current Fiscal Year stands at $4.28, this exact time last year, it stood at just $3.51.

That's a 22% upwards revision, which shows that Nvidia's growth can't only justify its forward multiples and allow it to grow into them relatively easily, but that the very estimates on which these forward multiples rely might actually be conservative. As you can see, consensus estimates have edged even further up following Wednesday's report. Given the recent release, I wouldn't be surprised if we see further upward revisions in the coming months as Wall Street analysts digest the numbers and update their estimates.

The Takeaway

To sum this up, it looks like Nvidia's Q1 earnings and Q2 guidance affirmed that any China-related concerns are not to be taken as seriously as many thought. The China export restrictions are a hurdle, but Nvidia's global demand, operational strength, and attractive valuation relative to its growth seem to present a compelling case to add to the stock at its current levels.

With consensus estimates rising post-earnings and likely to climb further, the market’s confidence in Nvidia is clear, which, along with a potential multiple expansion on even stronger EPS growth prospects, could drive excellent gains from here. And again, this is happening with or without China’s full participation. As Jensen Huang put it, AI is the future, and Nvidia is at the forefront. The numbers and the momentum back that up.

data.title: Global Ship Lease: Securing New Charters With Ongoing Deleverage
data.description:
Global Ship Lease (NYSE:GSL) was one of the few new positions we initiated this year, and in terms of performance, it has already exceeded our initial expectations. Since mid-April 2025, GSL's share price has increased by more than 25% (Fig. 1). Our strong buy rating recommendation was backed by 1) asset-light Strategy for GSL clients, 2) upside from Liquidation Value, 3) supportive OPEX strategy, and 4) a solid execution with downside protection based on a 10% dividend yield and a low single-digit P/E.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

GSL reported a solid set of numbers. Q1 beat estimates on higher revenues and earnings, reporting its highest TCE rate since 2018. In numbers, the company delivered an adjusted EPS of $2.65, coming in above Wall Street consensus estimates of $2.31. Looking at the Q1 results, GSL's adjusted EBITDA reached $132 million (Fig. 2). This supportive performance is due to higher-than-anticipated sales. Indeed, the company achieved a TCE rate of $30.75 per day, and its ships continued to secure solid charter rates.

Source: GSL Q1 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still Positive?

  1. GSL added $352 million to its top-line sales backlog, now totaling $1.87 billion (compared to its current market capitalization of $887 million). The average duration is now 2.3 years, and the company has extended the charter contracts on 19 ships. There were ships built in 2015. Additionally, the company has secured three new vessels, which are expected to generate an annual new EBITDA of €10 million each. Considering the latest vessels, FGLS secured 93% of contract coverage for this year and 75% in 2026. This provides downside protection and earnings visibility;
  2. The company's board of directors announced a dividend of $0.525 per share per quarter. This was above the previous quarterly payout of $0.45 per share. Key to report is that GSL still yields 8.5% on an annualized basis, and more importantly, this payout ratio represents just 20% of its current earnings;
  3. Additionally, GSL's cash position continues to increase, and the leverage ratio is declining, now below 1x (Fig. 3). Previously, we reported on how the company was actively managing its fleet. In Q1, GSL concluded its previously announced sale of three older container ships for $54.2 million, locking in a gain of $28.5 million for all three vessels. GSL's debt balance now amounts to $778 million, and with $428 million of cash, GSL's trailing net debt/EBITDA ratio has declined to just 0.9x. By year-end, the company's net debt will be 0.5 times its capital, and it is expected to achieve zero leverage by 2026;
  4. We support GSL's decarbonization strategy, and we believe that reducing emissions and improving energy efficiency will provide tangible earnings growth, combined with ESG benefits. Given its existing vessel fleet, the company will regain its liquidation value and continue to operate worldwide. On a higher level, the shipbuilding market remains concentrated in Asia, with a focus on China. According to Clarksons Research forecasts, the new ship orders declined by almost 30% in Q1. This is a long-standing tailwind for GSL contracts, tariffs, and book value.

Fig 3

Financials Estimate and Valuation

The company reported an adjusted EPS of $2.65, outperforming the Wall Street consensus estimates. Here at the Lab, we are also applying lower rates on GSL's interest expenses. This is due to FCF generation and deleverage, as well as a new loan with UBS that reduces the average cost of our debt. Given the addition of the three new ships, we have increased our FY 2025 sales from $670 million to $695 million, with direct consequences on the EBITDA level. Our new EBITDA reached $510 million. This is due to new contract lengths and higher visibility in the FY 2025 results. Going to the interest (expense and income), we arrived at negative results of $28 million, with a net income projection of $355 million and an EPS of $9.26.

Despite a 25% increase in stock price appreciation, GSL trades at a forward P/E of 2.69x with an EV/EBITDA of 2.2x. Continuing to apply a 3.6x P/E, aligned with the GSL historical average (Fig. 4), we increased our target price from $32.58 to $33.30 per share. We reiterated our firm conviction in the rating and continue to believe the company has revenue visibility, a solid order backlog, and a growing free cash flow (FCF). Even assuming a constant valuation multiple, with an ongoing delivery, GLS equity value will increase by $250 million per year.

Fig 4

Risks

Given our recent initiation of coverage, we report the following downside risks: an older fleet with higher maintenance costs, client concentration risks, charter rate volatility, increased regulatory requirements (future propulsion), and global events, such as pandemics, trade wars (Fig. 5), conflicts, and cybersecurity attacks. Approximately 85% of international trade goods are B2B, which means that tariff risks can disrupt shipping routes and affect supply and demand. An additional risk is associated with a declining value of container ships.

Fig 5

Conclusion

Global Ship Lease shares are compelling. Given the company's healthy financial position and substantial backlog, we raise our buy target to $33.30 per share.

data.title: J. M. Smucker: Sweet Yield And Free Cash Flow, Price Action Jammed
data.description:
Dividend Aristocrats have underperformed since the bull market began in October 2022. Among the worst-performing dividend-increase companies within the S&P 500 has been The J. M. Smucker Company (NYSE:SJM). The bears have eaten the bulls’ lunch over the past two years, despite SJM paying a sweet dividend, now near 4%.

I had a buy rating on the household-name Packaged Foods and Meats industry company within the Consumer Staples sector back in June 2024. Shares have actually fared fine, but the stock has been sandwiched between support and resistance.

Overall, dividends included, SJM is up 5.3% since my previous analysis, underperforming the SPX by just a few percentage points. I reiterate a buy rating on this high-yield, solid-value blue chip.

SJM Underperforming the Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL), Down Sharply Compared to the S&P 500 ETF (SPY)

Back in February, SJM reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $2.61 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $2.37, while revenue of $2.19 billion, down 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a modest $40 million miss. Shares were unchanged in the session that followed, continuing a general trading range on this low-beta, low-volatility Staples stock.

Looking closer at the quarter, the $11.8 billion market cap name offered updated guidance for its FY 2026. The management team pointed out that while its FY 2025 per-share earnings may be unchanged, better days could be on the horizon. Non-coffee earnings may grow above the firm’s long-term high single-digit target, but there’s uncertainty in not only commodity pricing but also how tariffs may impact the company going out several quarters.

Still, coffee profits were healthy in Smucker’s fiscal Q3. Eyes remain on how well Hostess, a recent acquisition, can be accretive to the bottom line, particularly with the “Make America Healthy Again” policy swing from last year into 2025. Smucker took a $1 billion impairment loss related to Hostess, but new leadership with that brand bodes well for the future.

Elsewhere, Uncrustables continues to be a big win. Full-year 2025 EPS is now seen in the range of $9.85 to $10.15 with top-line growth of 7.25%. Finally, the firm aims to reduce debt by $800 million this fiscal year, and that may help trim costs and boost margins.

On the earnings outlook, it’s steady as she goes. SJM is now in its fiscal 2025, and just modest bottom-line growth is seen through the out year, while sales growth slows from the relatively lofty 7%+ level in 2025 to under 2% next year. Hence, the price-to-earnings multiple is low. Still, the sell side is quite upbeat on the stock—there have been 15 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared with just a pair of downgrades.

Finally, profitability trends are sanguine—free cash flow per share is $7.67 over the past 12 months, resulting in a strong 6.8% FCF yield.

The J. M. Smucker Company: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

For a valuation refresh, if we assume normalized EPS of $10.10 over the next 12 months—near the current consensus—and apply a conservative 13x multiple, then shares should trade near $131. That’s down from a $145 target I had on the stock almost a year ago, and that reduction is due to softer earnings growth.

SJM: Very Low P/E with a High Dividend Yield vs History

Key risks include ongoing supply chain issues that impact operating performance and lead to higher costs. There are additional uncertainties with Hostess—the recent write-down was significant, and the onus is on the new leadership to make it a turnaround story. Of course, the ongoing trend toward GLP-1 weight-loss drugs could weigh on demand for Smucker’s snacks.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, June 10 BMO. Shares trade ex a $1.08 dividend on Monday, June 2, while the company’s Annual General Meeting occurs on Wednesday, August 13.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still sufficiently undervalued, SJM’s technical chart is not all that impressive. Notice in the chart below that shares have been trending lower since the start of last year. Recently, a trading range has emerged in the low $100s. I went long-term on the view, and I found that the RSI momentum oscillator using weekly price action is neutral at best. I’d like to see the weekly RSI jump through 60 along with a price breakout above $125.

But take a look at where long-term support appears—in the low $90s. The stock didn’t get all that close to those 2019 to early 2021 lows during the post-Liberation Day selloff, so that’s a positive. For now, there’s a high amount of shares traded up to $130, while a downtrend resistance line is apparent close to $120.

Overall, the technicals assert for more choppiness, so this is not an overly encouraging situation.

SJM: Shares Sideways Since 2024, Neutral RSI Trends

The Bottom Line

Despite the lukewarm chart, SJM’s decent valuation, high free cash flow, and big yield are attractive in today’s environment of a lofty S&P 500 P/E.

data.title: Edwards Lifesciences: Overbought Given Current Growth Prospects
data.description:
Today, we put Edwards Lifesciences Corporation (EW) in the spotlight. The stock in this large medical device concern has slowly ground higher since a big decline last summer. The stock got a bit of a lift earlier this week when competitor Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) announced it was going to end global sales of its ACURATE neo2 and ACURATE Prime aortic valve systems due to regulatory feedback. How does the rest of 2025 play out for Edward Lifesciences? An analysis follows below.

Edwards Lifesciences Corporation is headquartered in Irvine, CA. The company is focused on developing, manufacturing and distributing products and technologies to treat advanced cardiovascular disease. The company's product portfolio includes various heart valves and related items used during cardiovascular surgeries. The stock currently trades just north of $78.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $45 billion.

The company has several business segments. The fastest growing of these is Transcatheter Mitral and Tricuspid Therapies or TMTT. This division is focused on treating patients with tricuspid and mitral valve diseases with products like EVOQUE, SAPIEN M3, and PASCAL. The much larger but slow-growth business division at Edwards is Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement or TAVR powered by products like the SAPIEN 3 Ultra RESILIA platform.

The company's Surgical business segment is smaller than TAVR and is seeing similar slow sales growth. It consists of products like MITRIS, INSPIRIS, and KONECT.

Recent Results:

Edwards Lifesciences Corporation posted their Q1 numbers on April 23rd. The company delivered non-GAAP earnings of 64 cents a share, four pennies a share above the consensus. R&D expenses came in at 18% of revenues or $255 million, down from 19.6% of sales in 1Q2024. Overall, gross margins improved from 78.4% to 78.7%.

Revenues rose six percent on a year-over-year basis to $1.41 billion, besting expectations by $10 million. TAVR revenues increased 3.8% from the same period a year ago to $1.05 billion, 5.4% on a constant currency basis. The Surgical segment saw revenues rise one percent to $251 million, three percent on a constant currency basis. MITRIS just launched in China and KONECT should be approved in Europe by yearend. TMTT sales rose 58% to $115 million, just over 60% on a constant currency basis. The SAPIEN M3 also recently received the CE mark to be able to be marketed in Europe. Management has projected it believes TMTT revenues can reach $2 billion by FY2030.

Management raised FY2025 sales guidance to $5.7 billion to $6.1 billion from $5.6 billion to $6 billion previously. TMTT FY2025 sales guidance was raised to $550 million from $530 million previously. Overall sales guidance was also increased due to favorable currency impacts. Leadership kept its EPS guidance for FY2025 of between $2.40 to $2.50 a share. As for tariffs, management stated they probably will mean an approximate $0.05 a share hit to EPS based upon the tariffs that are already in place.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Analyst firms are somewhat split on the current prospects around Edward Lifesciences Corporation right now. Since first quarter results hit the wires, seven analyst firms including Jefferies and Leerink Partners have reissued Hold ratings on the stock. All four analyst firms that gave price targets had identical $75 a share targets. Five analyst firms including Citigroup and William Blair maintained Buy ratings on the stock. Two of these had minor upward price target revisions. Price targets from the optimists range from $83 to $95 a share.

Just over one percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Several insiders were frequent and consistent sellers of the stock in 2024. That insider behavior has continued into 2025. So far this year, insiders have sold just over $7 million worth of shares collectively.

Edwards Lifesciences Corporation listed nearly $3.1 billion in cash and marketable securities on its 10-K it filed for the first quarter against just under $600 million in long-term debt. Edwards bought back $300 million of its own stock and has $1 billion left on an existing stock buyback authorization plan.

Conclusion:

Edward Lifesciences Corporation made $2.43 a share (non-GAAP) on $5.44 billion in revenue in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits rising very slightly in FY2025 to $2.47 a share as revenues rise to $5.89 billion.
